Sustainable disposable chitosan dip-coated paper-based sorbent for 
extraction of sunset yellow from food samples and pharmaceuticals

Eman El-Zayat , Sherin F. Hammad , Samah F. El-Malla , Aya A. Abdella *

Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Egypt

A R T I C L E I N F O

A B S T R A C T

Keywords:
Chitosan
Sunset yellow
Paper-based extraction
Sustainable sorbent
Solid phase extraction

Sunset yellow (SY) is a synthetic azo dye that has been used widely in food industries and is linked to numerous 
health problems, especially with children. In this work, a straightforward, affordable, and eco-friendly paper- 
based extraction method was devised for SY extraction and subsequent spectrophotometric determination. The 
extraction method relied on the use of a disposable and biodegradable chitosan (CHT)-modified paper-based 
sorbent. The prepared sorptive sheets were characterized using scanning electron microscopy, energy- 
dispersive X-ray analysis, and water contact angle measurements. SY adsorption onto CHT was best described 
by the pseudo-first order kinetic model. The different parameters governing the extraction efficiency of the 
devised paper-based sorbent were investigated. SY was most effectively captured on paper treated with 0.6% 
CHT after 20 min and was released using a mixture of methanol and 0.1 N HCl (1:1, v/v) after 25 min, achieving 
an enrichment factor of 2.4. The absorbance intensity at 482 nm showed a good correlation with SY concen-
tration over the range of 10–60 μg/mL. The devised approach showed recovery values between 99 – 105% and 
enabled the quantification of SY in spiked orange juice, spiked carrot juice, pediatric syrup and carbonated 
orange beverage without the need for specific reagents, costly instrumentation, or tedious extraction processes. 
The AGREEprep tool demonstrated the superior environmental impact of the developed method compared to the 
reported procedures for cloud point extraction.

1. Introduction

A wide variety of natural and synthetic coloring agents are being 
added to numerous food and pharmaceutical products to augment color 
qualities, enhance the aesthetic, and preserve the original attractiveness 
of food [1]. The food colorant industry is expanding at an annual rate of 
4.6 % and is projected to attain a value of 2.3 billion dollars in the global 
market. The color of food is regarded as the most crucial factor when 
purchasing any food item, as it is typically linked to the freshness and 
quality of the product [1]. However, numerous colorants can induce 
severe negative effects, including cancer and allergic reactions [2]. Strict 
regulations governing the utilization of approved colorants and the 
disclosure of their origins minimize health hazards associated with such 
toxic compounds, thereby safeguarding consumer safety and preserving 
product integrity through careful control of the extraction and analysis 
of food colorants [2].

Sunset yellow (SY, E110), an orange-red colored synthetic azo-dye, is 
one of the most widely used water-soluble coloring agents that has been 

used extensively in pharmaceutical and food industries. (Fig. S1A). The 
acceptable daily intake of SY is 4 mg/kg/day as decided by European 
food safety authority [3]. The ingestion of SY has been linked to some 
adverse health effects such as sensitivity reaction, hyperactivity, cellular 
immunosuppression, asthma, genetic material damage, hepatotoxicity, 
renal failure, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and po-
tential immunotoxicity [4]. Therefore, a straightforward approach is 
necessary to control SY level in the different products.

So far, some methods have been developed for determination of SY in 
food samples. These methods include electrochemical [5], chromato-
graphic [6–8], and spectroscopic methods [9–10]. Due to the complex 
nature of sample matrix, SY is usually extracted using cloud point 
extraction [11], liquid-liquid extraction (LLE) [12], and column solid 
phase extraction (SPE) [9]. However, the utilization of hazardous sol-
vents and persistent toxic organic waste make LLE and conventional SPE 
harmful to the ecosystem. Also, SPE requires well-trained personnel and 
expensive instruments. On the other hand, cloud point extraction is 
laborious and suffers from impaired reproducibility due to possible loss

of the extractive surfactant layer during phase separation [13]. Conse-
quently, it is imperative to develop a simple, fast, and sustainable 
method for extraction recovery of SY from various samples. Thus, the 
costly equipment, time-consuming, and tedious extraction processes are 
substituted.

The introduction of paper-based sorbents is significant milestone in 
real-time pharmaceutical quality control and food safety assessment 
[14]. Owing to their numerous excellent capabilities, paper-based 
sorptive extraction has gained a lot of attention. These characteristics 
include being cost-effective, biodegradable and biocompatible, versa-
tile, and producing a quick response [15]. The progress of paper-based 
SPE is ascribed to its simplicity, sustainability, and affordability. Also, 
the use of existing planar material greatly simplifies the preparation of 
sorptive phases. On the other hand, the natural origin of cellulose con-
tributes to the greenness of these sorbents by affording high wettability, 
reducing the environmental hazards, and producing biodegradable 
wastes [16].

The preparation of paper-based sorbents can be performed through 
physical or chemical modification of paper’s surface with polymer [17], 
employing either dip-coating [18–19], filtration [20], or drop-casting 
[21] strategies. The dip-coating technique is based on paper immer-
sion in the coating solution for a specified time; thereafter, the excess 
coating solution is removed. In contrast, the drop-casting method in-
volves putting a drop of the coating agent directly on the paper’s surface, 
while the filtration method works by soaking the surface modifier into 
the paper’s porous surface as the coating solution is filtered.

Chitosan (CHT) (Fig. S1B) is a linear polysaccharide composed of 
varying amounts of (β1→4) linked N-acetyl-2 amino-2-deoxy-D-glucose 
(glucosamine) and 2-amino-2-deoxy-D-Glucose (N-acetyl-glucosamine) 
residues [22]. CHT is soluble in aqueous acidic media via primary amine 
protonation [23,24]. Also, in acidic medium, CHT has a considerable 
affinity towards acid dyes due to the protonated amine groups. CHT is a 
well-known polysaccharide for its ability to be cast as thin films that is 
characterized by its excellent adsorption capabilities [25]. However, the 
fragility of CHT films is a major concern affecting their integrity. 
Numerous researchers have utilized different plasticizers for maintain-
ing the flexibility and integrity of CHT membranes [26]. However, such 
chemical modifiers would lower CHT’s adsorption abilities. Thus, the 
deposition of CHT on a paper support can alleviate most of the obstacles 
encountered [27].

In this work, a novel paper-based SPE method was developed for 
extraction and spectrophotometric determination of SY. The developed 
method relies on the use of CHT-coated paper sorbent. Due to the pro-
tonated CHT, SY was effectively adsorbed on the newly formed sorbent 
in a short time allowing its determination using spectrophotometry after 
elution using an aliquot of 1 mL of elution solvent. The devised CHT- 
coated sorbent was successfully applied for SY determination in spiked 
orange juice, spiked carrot juice, pediatric syrup, and carbonated 
beverage. The developed method was proved to be green, rapid, and 
straightforward, outperforming the performance of the reported SY 
extraction methods.

2. Experimental

2.1. Materials

CHT(med. MW, Deacetylation degree of 75–85 %) was purchased 
from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Glacial acetic acid (HPLC Grade) 
was purchased from Advent Chembio (Maharashtra, India). Double 
Rings® (102) filter paper (9.0 cm diameter) was used for paper-based 
sorbent preparation. Sodium hydroxide (Laboratory Reagent Grade) 
was supplied by ISO–CHEM (Egypt). SY with purity 99 % was kindly 
supplied by sigma pharmaceutical industry (Quesna, Egypt). HPLC 
grade methanol and hydrochloric acid were obtained from Sigma- 
Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Pediatric analgesic syrup labelled to contain 
50 mg/mL paracetamol, a product of EIPICO (Tenth of Ramadan City, 

2 

Egypt), was purchased from local pharmacy (Tanta, Egypt). Carbonated 
orange beverage was obtained from local market (Tanta, Egypt). Fresh 
oranges and carrots were purchased from the local market, washed 
thoroughly with water, and processed to obtain fresh orange and carrot 
juice. Orange juice was produced by pressing fresh oranges, whereas 
carrot juice was made by blending boiled carrots with a hand blender. 
Both juices were strained to remove any particulate matter and stored in 
clean containers at 4 

C until used.

◦

2.2. Instruments and software

UV/Vis spectra were recorded in the range of 300–600 nm on a 
JASCO v-530 UV/Vis double beam spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) 
equipped with a 1-mL microcuvette and connected to a PC operated by 
Jasco Spectra Manager (1.55.00). A JOEL JSM IT-00 scanning electron 
microscope (Tokyo, Japan) was used for acquiring SEM images and 
energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX-SEM) analysis. For water contact angle 
(WCA) measurements, a smartphone (Samsung Galaxy note 10 lite, 
Vietnam) setup was employed for photo acquisition [28]. Images of a 
100-μL water drop were captured using the single take mode (15 s), then 
transferred to a PC for processing with ImageJ 1.54 g (version 
1.8.0_345). The contact angle was measured using the Drop Analysis 
Plug-In [29], following these steps: open ImageJ, load the image, 
navigate to Image -> Type->32-bit, Plug-in- > drop-analysis - > Drop 
analysis-LB-ADSA. Hot plate magnetic stirrer (DAIHAN, Malaysia) and 
digital balance (Sartorius, Germany) were used.

2.3. Preparation of paper-based sorbent

The CHT-coated paper-based sorbent was prepared according the 
method reported by Abdella et al. [28] that were modified after Tang 
et al. [30]. Each paper piece was dipped in 5 mL of 0.6 % CHT, dissolved 
acetic acid (1 % v/v), for 2 h. The paper sheet was positioned upright to 
aid in the removal of excess CHT and was left to air dry at room tem-
◦
perature (25 
C). Then, the CHT-coated paper was cut into small pieces 
(1 × 1 cm) and stored in a dry clean container until further utilized, as 
shown in Fig. 1.

2.4. General procedure for sunset yellow determination

A stock standard solution of SY (1 mg/mL) was prepared in distilled 
water. Serial dilutions were made using the same solvent to obtain 
working standard solutions of SY between 10 - 60 μg/mL. The prepared 
paper-based sorbent sheets were placed in 2 mL of working standard SY 
solution for 20 min. Then, the sorptive paper pieces were moved to a dry 
paper sheet for 1 min and then immersed in 1 mL of solvent system 
composed of methanol: 0.1 N HCl (1:1 v/v) for 25 min (Fig. 1). Finally, 
the UV/Vis spectra of the eluate solutions were recorded between 300 – 
600 nm. Calibration curves were constructed by plotting the absorbance 
at 482 nm against the corresponding dye concentration (μg/mL).

2.5. Determination of sunset yellow in real samples

For fresh juice analysis, the homemade orange and carrot juice were 
spiked with SY. Different aliquots (0–500 μL) of 1 mg/mL stock standard 
solution of SY were separately transferred into a series of 10-mL volu-
metric flasks. The solution was made up to final volume using each juice 
to obtain spiked juice samples containing 0–50 μg/mL of SY. The solu-
tions were analyzed as per the procedure aforementioned in Section 2.4
to calculate the recovered SY concentration.

For real samples, containers of pediatric antipyretic syrup and 
carbonated orange beverage, were shaken for homogenization. An 
aliquot of 1 mL of each sample was separately transferred into a series of 
tube vessels. Then, a volume of 1 mL of standard SY (0–50 μg/mL) was 
added to each vessel. The analysis was performed as aforementioned in 
Section 2.4. SY concentration in the diluted sample solution was

3. Results and discussion

3.1. Preparation and characterization

Since the CHT layer is fundamental for SY binding onto paper sur-
face, the effectiveness of coating process was investigated using various 
characterization techniques. To prepare the paper sorbent, the paper 
was dip-coated with 0.6 % CHT for 2 h. The prepared sorptive sheets 
were then soaked in SY to evaluate the feasibility of the extraction 
process.

SEM images were acquired for uncoated paper (Fig. 2A and D), pa-
per’s surface after coating with CHT (Fig. 2B and E), and coated paper 
after SY binding (Fig. 2C and F). The unmodified paper showed a porous 

surface displaying fibrous cellulose structure that was changed to 
smoother surface with less voids after coating with CHT. The deposition 
of CHT on paper’s surface could be attributed to the electrostatic 
interaction between the negative charges of cellulose and the positively 
charged CHT [28]. Besides, the binding of SY to paper’s surface 
increased the surface roughness and irregularities, as demonstrated by 
the SEM images (Fig. 2C and F) [31].

To confirm the effectiveness of the coating step, elemental analysis 
was done on the uncoated cellulose fiber surface, paper coated with CHT 
and coated paper after SY binding (Fig. S2). The elemental composition 
of the uncoated paper revealed carbon and oxygen as the primary ele-
ments (Fig. S2A). While the CHT-coated paper showed a nitrogen 
component originating from amine groups of CHT (Fig. S2B). This 
would suggest effective paper coating with a CHT layer. Also, an addi-
tional sulfur component was found in coated paper after adsorption of 
SY indicating effective binding (Fig. S2C).

Fig. 2. SEM images of uncoated paper (A, D), CHT-coated paper (B, E), and CHT-coated paper after SY binding (C, F) at 20 μm and 5 μm.

Furthermore, the WCA was measured for sorptive sheets coated with 
different CHT concentrations (0.2–1.2 %). As shown in Fig. S3, by 
increasing the concentration of CHT in the coating solution, the WCA 
increased, indicating the effective coating of paper surface even at low 
CHT concentrations. WCA measures the wettability of paper; higher 
contact angle indicates bad wettability of paper and weak adsorption. 
Thus, WCA is a crucial parameter that should be considered during the 
optimization of coating solution concentration.

Additionally, the binding kinetics of SY, at three different concen-
trations (20, 30, and 40 μg/mL) to CHT-coated paper surface was 
studied through the fitting of the binding capacity (Eq (1)) to both 
linearized pseudo-first order and second order kinetics models, repre-
sented by Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively. Previous studies on acid dyes 
binding onto CHT reported pseudo-first order as the best fit model [32]. 
Consistently, SY adsorption onto CHT was best described by pseudo-first 
order kinetic model (Fig. S4). This was indicated by closeness of 
calculated equilibrium binding capacity (Qe) to the experimental value 
as well as the high r2 (Table 1).

3.2. Optimization of sorbent preparation and performance

The different experimental parameters governing SY binding onto 
the CHT-coated paper were optimized. Since CHT is responsible for SY 
binding on paper’s surface, the concentration of CHT coating solution 
was studied to find out the optimum concentration that achieve 
maximum adsorption capacity without impairing paper’s wettability 
[33]. Different concentrations of CHT, ranging from 0.2 % to 1.2 %, 
were tested and a blank, uncoated paper, served as a negative control. 
The binding capacity of the sorptive sheet was markedly increased after 
coating with CHT, reaching a plateau between 0.2 and 0.8 %. Beyond 
this point, a decrease in binding efficiency was noticed. This can be 
ascribed to increased hydrophobicity and wettability resulting from the 
dense CHT coat, as indicated by WCA > 90 (Fig. S3) [34]. The highest 
possible CHT concentration (0.6 %) was chosen as it could achieve high 
binding affinity without impairing surface wettability, maintaining 
method’s robustness (Fig. 3A).

coated paper surface increased with time reaching a equilibrium after 24 
min. Thus, a contact time of 20 min was chosen to avoid the loss of 
discrimination ability that is usually observed with sorbents beyond 
their maximum binding capacity.

After finding out the optimum conditions for SY binding, the 
different experimental parameters affecting its desorption from the 
CHT-coated paper sheet were studied. The effect of elution solvent was 
studied by placing CHT-coated sheets with SY bound onto their surface 
in 1 mL of different elution solvents for 20 min. The eluted SY was 
spectrophotometrically detected at 482 nm (Fig. 3C). An elution solvent 
composed of a mixture of methanol: 0.1 N HCl (1:1, v/v) could attain the 
highest desorption ability of SY from the CHT-coated paper. This sug-
gested the contribution of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces in 
SY binding to the CHT-modified paper surface.

To assess the effect of time on the elution efficiency of the bound SY, 
the contact time was investigated between 5 and 30 min. The absor-
bance of the eluate recorded at 482 nm was monitored as a function of 
time (Fig. 3D). A contact time of 25 min was chosen as it resulted in the 
highest elution efficiency and hence absorbance values.

At optimal conditions, the developed CHT-coated paper sorbent 
achieved an enrichment factor (EF) of 2.4 in the first extraction cycle, 
calculated using Eq (4), indicating the ability of the sorbent sheet to 
enrich SY. This showed the significant impact of the CHT coating on the 
sensitivity of SY determination. The reusability of the sorptive sheets 
was studied to determine the number of cycles that could be applied 
without losing extraction efficiency and enrichment ability. The used 
paper sheets were thoroughly washed with distilled water in three 
successive cycles of 1 mL each, between extraction cycles. As shown in 
Fig. 3E, a significant decrease in the EF was observed after the first cycle. 
This decrease could be attributed to a potential partial loss of the coating 
layer, which would impair method’s sensitivity. Therefore, it is recom-
mended to use the developed sorbent for only one cycle.

3.3. Method validation

(Eq 4) 

The validation of the paper-based sorptive extraction method was 
performed in accordance with the ICH-Q2 (R1) guidelines. Under opti-
mum conditions, eluate absorbance at 482 nm increased quantitively 
with increasing SY concentration (Fig. 4A). The method demonstrated a 
good linearity (R2 = 0996) in the range of 10 – 60 μg/mL (Fig. 4B). The 
regression parameters were calculated and presented in Table 2. 
Method’s accuracy was evaluated at three different concentration levels, 
and good accuracy was indicated by a mean percentage recovery within 
100 ± 5 % (Table S1). Moreover, a %RSD of less than 5 % for the tested 
concentration levels demonstrated the intraday and inter-day precision 
of the developed method (Table S1).

Numerous researchers have utilized different plasticizers for maintain-
ing the flexibility and integrity of CHT membranes [26]. However, such 
chemical modifiers would lower CHT’s adsorption abilities. Thus, the 
deposition of CHT on a paper support can alleviate most of the obstacles 
encountered [27].

In this work, a novel paper-based SPE method was developed for 
extraction and spectrophotometric determination of SY. The developed 
method relies on the use of CHT-coated paper sorbent. Due to the pro-
tonated CHT, SY was effectively adsorbed on the newly formed sorbent 
in a short time allowing its determination using spectrophotometry after 
elution using an aliquot of 1 mL of elution solvent. The devised CHT- 
coated sorbent was successfully applied for SY determination in spiked 
orange juice, spiked carrot juice, pediatric syrup, and carbonated 
beverage. The developed method was proved to be green, rapid, and 
straightforward, outperforming the performance of the reported SY 
extraction methods.

2.1. Materials

3. Results and discussion

3.1. Preparation and characterization

Since the CHT layer is fundamental for SY binding onto paper sur-
face, the effectiveness of coating process was investigated using various 
characterization techniques. To prepare the paper sorbent, the paper 
was dip-coated with 0.6 % CHT for 2 h. The prepared sorptive sheets 
were then soaked in SY to evaluate the feasibility of the extraction 
process.

SEM images were acquired for uncoated paper (Fig. 2A and D), pa-
per’s surface after coating with CHT (Fig. 2B and E), and coated paper 
after SY binding (Fig. 2C and F). The unmodified paper showed a porous 

surface displaying fibrous cellulose structure that was changed to 
smoother surface with less voids after coating with CHT. The deposition 
of CHT on paper’s surface could be attributed to the electrostatic 
interaction between the negative charges of cellulose and the positively 
charged CHT [28]. Besides, the binding of SY to paper’s surface 
increased the surface roughness and irregularities, as demonstrated by 
the SEM images (Fig. 2C and F) [31].

To confirm the effectiveness of the coating step, elemental analysis 
was done on the uncoated cellulose fiber surface, paper coated with CHT 
and coated paper after SY binding (Fig. S2). The elemental composition 
of the uncoated paper revealed carbon and oxygen as the primary ele-
ments (Fig. S2A). While the CHT-coated paper showed a nitrogen 
component originating from amine groups of CHT (Fig. S2B). This 
would suggest effective paper coating with a CHT layer. Also, an addi-
tional sulfur component was found in coated paper after adsorption of 
SY indicating effective binding (Fig. S2C).

3.2. Optimization of sorbent preparation and performance

The different experimental parameters governing SY binding onto 
the CHT-coated paper were optimized. Since CHT is responsible for SY 
binding on paper’s surface, the concentration of CHT coating solution 
was studied to find out the optimum concentration that achieve 
maximum adsorption capacity without impairing paper’s wettability 
[33]. Different concentrations of CHT, ranging from 0.2 % to 1.2 %, 
were tested and a blank, uncoated paper, served as a negative control. 
The binding capacity of the sorptive sheet was markedly increased after 
coating with CHT, reaching a plateau between 0.2 and 0.8 %. Beyond 
this point, a decrease in binding efficiency was noticed. This can be 
ascribed to increased hydrophobicity and wettability resulting from the 
dense CHT coat, as indicated by WCA > 90 (Fig. S3) [34]. The highest 
possible CHT concentration (0.6 %) was chosen as it could achieve high 
binding affinity without impairing surface wettability, maintaining 
method’s robustness (Fig. 3A).

coated paper surface increased with time reaching a equilibrium after 24 
min. Thus, a contact time of 20 min was chosen to avoid the loss of 
discrimination ability that is usually observed with sorbents beyond 
their maximum binding capacity.

After finding out the optimum conditions for SY binding, the 
different experimental parameters affecting its desorption from the 
CHT-coated paper sheet were studied. The effect of elution solvent was 
studied by placing CHT-coated sheets with SY bound onto their surface 
in 1 mL of different elution solvents for 20 min. The eluted SY was 
spectrophotometrically detected at 482 nm (Fig. 3C). An elution solvent 
composed of a mixture of methanol: 0.1 N HCl (1:1, v/v) could attain the 
highest desorption ability of SY from the CHT-coated paper. This sug-
gested the contribution of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces in 
SY binding to the CHT-modified paper surface.

To assess the effect of time on the elution efficiency of the bound SY, 
the contact time was investigated between 5 and 30 min. The absor-
bance of the eluate recorded at 482 nm was monitored as a function of 
time (Fig. 3D). A contact time of 25 min was chosen as it resulted in the 
highest elution efficiency and hence absorbance values.

At optimal conditions, the developed CHT-coated paper sorbent 
achieved an enrichment factor (EF) of 2.4 in the first extraction cycle, 
calculated using Eq (4), indicating the ability of the sorbent sheet to 
enrich SY. This showed the significant impact of the CHT coating on the 
sensitivity of SY determination. The reusability of the sorptive sheets 
was studied to determine the number of cycles that could be applied 
without losing extraction efficiency and enrichment ability. The used 
paper sheets were thoroughly washed with distilled water in three 
successive cycles of 1 mL each, between extraction cycles. As shown in 
Fig. 3E, a significant decrease in the EF was observed after the first cycle. 
This decrease could be attributed to a potential partial loss of the coating 
layer, which would impair method’s sensitivity. Therefore, it is recom-
mended to use the developed sorbent for only one cycle.

extraction of sunset yellow from food samples and pharmaceuticals

Eman El-Zayat , Sherin F. Hammad , Samah F. El-Malla , Aya A. Abdella *

A B S T R A C T

Keywords:
Chitosan
Sunset yellow
Paper-based extraction
Sustainable sorbent
Solid phase extraction

Sunset yellow (SY) is a synthetic azo dye that has been used widely in food industries and is linked to numerous 
health problems, especially with children. In this work, a straightforward, affordable, and eco-friendly paper- 
based extraction method was devised for SY extraction and subsequent spectrophotometric determination. The 
extraction method relied on the use of a disposable and biodegradable chitosan (CHT)-modified paper-based 
sorbent. The prepared sorptive sheets were characterized using scanning electron microscopy, energy- 
dispersive X-ray analysis, and water contact angle measurements. SY adsorption onto CHT was best described 
by the pseudo-first order kinetic model. The different parameters governing the extraction efficiency of the 
devised paper-based sorbent were investigated. SY was most effectively captured on paper treated with 0.6% 
CHT after 20 min and was released using a mixture of methanol and 0.1 N HCl (1:1, v/v) after 25 min, achieving 
an enrichment factor of 2.4. The absorbance intensity at 482 nm showed a good correlation with SY concen-
tration over the range of 10–60 μg/mL. The devised approach showed recovery values between 99 – 105% and 
enabled the quantification of SY in spiked orange juice, spiked carrot juice, pediatric syrup and carbonated 
orange beverage without the need for specific reagents, costly instrumentation, or tedious extraction processes. 
The AGREEprep tool demonstrated the superior environmental impact of the developed method compared to the 
reported procedures for cloud point extraction.

1. Introduction

A wide variety of natural and synthetic coloring agents are being 
added to numerous food and pharmaceutical products to augment color 
qualities, enhance the aesthetic, and preserve the original attractiveness 
of food [1]. The food colorant industry is expanding at an annual rate of 
4.6 % and is projected to attain a value of 2.3 billion dollars in the global 
market. The color of food is regarded as the most crucial factor when 
purchasing any food item, as it is typically linked to the freshness and 
quality of the product [1]. However, numerous colorants can induce 
severe negative effects, including cancer and allergic reactions [2]. Strict 
regulations governing the utilization of approved colorants and the 
disclosure of their origins minimize health hazards associated with such 
toxic compounds, thereby safeguarding consumer safety and preserving 
product integrity through careful control of the extraction and analysis 
of food colorants [2].

Sunset yellow (SY, E110), an orange-red colored synthetic azo-dye, is 
one of the most widely used water-soluble coloring agents that has been 

used extensively in pharmaceutical and food industries. (Fig. S1A). The 
acceptable daily intake of SY is 4 mg/kg/day as decided by European 
food safety authority [3]. The ingestion of SY has been linked to some 
adverse health effects such as sensitivity reaction, hyperactivity, cellular 
immunosuppression, asthma, genetic material damage, hepatotoxicity, 
renal failure, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and po-
tential immunotoxicity [4]. Therefore, a straightforward approach is 
necessary to control SY level in the different products.

So far, some methods have been developed for determination of SY in 
food samples. These methods include electrochemical [5], chromato-
graphic [6–8], and spectroscopic methods [9–10]. Due to the complex 
nature of sample matrix, SY is usually extracted using cloud point 
extraction [11], liquid-liquid extraction (LLE) [12], and column solid 
phase extraction (SPE) [9]. However, the utilization of hazardous sol-
vents and persistent toxic organic waste make LLE and conventional SPE 
harmful to the ecosystem. Also, SPE requires well-trained personnel and 
expensive instruments. On the other hand, cloud point extraction is 
laborious and suffers from impaired reproducibility due to possible loss

quently, it is imperative to develop a simple, fast, and sustainable 
method for extraction recovery of SY from various samples. Thus, the 
costly equipment, time-consuming, and tedious extraction processes are 
substituted.

The introduction of paper-based sorbents is significant milestone in 
real-time pharmaceutical quality control and food safety assessment 
[14]. Owing to their numerous excellent capabilities, paper-based 
sorptive extraction has gained a lot of attention. These characteristics 
include being cost-effective, biodegradable and biocompatible, versa-
tile, and producing a quick response [15]. The progress of paper-based 
SPE is ascribed to its simplicity, sustainability, and affordability. Also, 
the use of existing planar material greatly simplifies the preparation of 
sorptive phases. On the other hand, the natural origin of cellulose con-
tributes to the greenness of these sorbents by affording high wettability, 
reducing the environmental hazards, and producing biodegradable 
wastes [16].

The preparation of paper-based sorbents can be performed through 
physical or chemical modification of paper’s surface with polymer [17], 
employing either dip-coating [18–19], filtration [20], or drop-casting 
[21] strategies. The dip-coating technique is based on paper immer-
sion in the coating solution for a specified time; thereafter, the excess 
coating solution is removed. In contrast, the drop-casting method in-
volves putting a drop of the coating agent directly on the paper’s surface, 
while the filtration method works by soaking the surface modifier into 
the paper’s porous surface as the coating solution is filtered.

Chitosan (CHT) (Fig. S1B) is a linear polysaccharide composed of 
varying amounts of (β1→4) linked N-acetyl-2 amino-2-deoxy-D-glucose 
(glucosamine) and 2-amino-2-deoxy-D-Glucose (N-acetyl-glucosamine) 
residues [22]. CHT is soluble in aqueous acidic media via primary amine 
protonation [23,24]. Also, in acidic medium, CHT has a considerable 
affinity towards acid dyes due to the protonated amine groups. CHT is a 
well-known polysaccharide for its ability to be cast as thin films that is 
characterized by its excellent adsorption capabilities [25]. However, the 
fragility of CHT films is a major concern affecting their integrity. 
Numerous researchers have utilized different plasticizers for maintain-
ing the flexibility and integrity of CHT membranes [26]. However, such 
chemical modifiers would lower CHT’s adsorption abilities. Thus, the 
deposition of CHT on a paper support can alleviate most of the obstacles 
encountered [27].

In this work, a novel paper-based SPE method was developed for 
extraction and spectrophotometric determination of SY. The developed 
method relies on the use of CHT-coated paper sorbent. Due to the pro-
tonated CHT, SY was effectively adsorbed on the newly formed sorbent 
in a short time allowing its determination using spectrophotometry after 
elution using an aliquot of 1 mL of elution solvent. The devised CHT- 
coated sorbent was successfully applied for SY determination in spiked 
orange juice, spiked carrot juice, pediatric syrup, and carbonated 
beverage. The developed method was proved to be green, rapid, and 
straightforward, outperforming the performance of the reported SY 
extraction methods.

3.2. Optimization of sorbent preparation and performance

The different experimental parameters governing SY binding onto 
the CHT-coated paper were optimized. Since CHT is responsible for SY 
binding on paper’s surface, the concentration of CHT coating solution 
was studied to find out the optimum concentration that achieve 
maximum adsorption capacity without impairing paper’s wettability 
[33]. Different concentrations of CHT, ranging from 0.2 % to 1.2 %, 
were tested and a blank, uncoated paper, served as a negative control. 
The binding capacity of the sorptive sheet was markedly increased after 
coating with CHT, reaching a plateau between 0.2 and 0.8 %. Beyond 
this point, a decrease in binding efficiency was noticed. This can be 
ascribed to increased hydrophobicity and wettability resulting from the 
dense CHT coat, as indicated by WCA > 90 (Fig. S3) [34]. The highest 
possible CHT concentration (0.6 %) was chosen as it could achieve high 
binding affinity without impairing surface wettability, maintaining 
method’s robustness (Fig. 3A).

coated paper surface increased with time reaching a equilibrium after 24 
min. Thus, a contact time of 20 min was chosen to avoid the loss of 
discrimination ability that is usually observed with sorbents beyond 
their maximum binding capacity.

After finding out the optimum conditions for SY binding, the 
different experimental parameters affecting its desorption from the 
CHT-coated paper sheet were studied. The effect of elution solvent was 
studied by placing CHT-coated sheets with SY bound onto their surface 
in 1 mL of different elution solvents for 20 min. The eluted SY was 
spectrophotometrically detected at 482 nm (Fig. 3C). An elution solvent 
composed of a mixture of methanol: 0.1 N HCl (1:1, v/v) could attain the 
highest desorption ability of SY from the CHT-coated paper. This sug-
gested the contribution of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces in 
SY binding to the CHT-modified paper surface.

To assess the effect of time on the elution efficiency of the bound SY, 
the contact time was investigated between 5 and 30 min. The absor-
bance of the eluate recorded at 482 nm was monitored as a function of 
time (Fig. 3D). A contact time of 25 min was chosen as it resulted in the 
highest elution efficiency and hence absorbance values.

At optimal conditions, the developed CHT-coated paper sorbent 
achieved an enrichment factor (EF) of 2.4 in the first extraction cycle, 
calculated using Eq (4), indicating the ability of the sorbent sheet to 
enrich SY. This showed the significant impact of the CHT coating on the 
sensitivity of SY determination. The reusability of the sorptive sheets 
was studied to determine the number of cycles that could be applied 
without losing extraction efficiency and enrichment ability. The used 
paper sheets were thoroughly washed with distilled water in three 
successive cycles of 1 mL each, between extraction cycles. As shown in 
Fig. 3E, a significant decrease in the EF was observed after the first cycle. 
This decrease could be attributed to a potential partial loss of the coating 
layer, which would impair method’s sensitivity. Therefore, it is recom-
mended to use the developed sorbent for only one cycle.

Sustainable disposable chitosan dip-coated paper-based sorbent for 
extraction of sunset yellow from food samples and pharmaceuticals

Eman El-Zayat , Sherin F. Hammad , Samah F. El-Malla , Aya A. Abdella *

Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Egypt

A R T I C L E I N F O

A B S T R A C T

Keywords:
Chitosan
Sunset yellow
Paper-based extraction
Sustainable sorbent
Solid phase extraction

Sunset yellow (SY) is a synthetic azo dye that has been used widely in food industries and is linked to numerous 
health problems, especially with children. In this work, a straightforward, affordable, and eco-friendly paper- 
based extraction method was devised for SY extraction and subsequent spectrophotometric determination. The 
extraction method relied on the use of a disposable and biodegradable chitosan (CHT)-modified paper-based 
sorbent. The prepared sorptive sheets were characterized using scanning electron microscopy, energy- 
dispersive X-ray analysis, and water contact angle measurements. SY adsorption onto CHT was best described 
by the pseudo-first order kinetic model. The different parameters governing the extraction efficiency of the 
devised paper-based sorbent were investigated. SY was most effectively captured on paper treated with 0.6% 
CHT after 20 min and was released using a mixture of methanol and 0.1 N HCl (1:1, v/v) after 25 min, achieving 
an enrichment factor of 2.4. The absorbance intensity at 482 nm showed a good correlation with SY concen-
tration over the range of 10–60 μg/mL. The devised approach showed recovery values between 99 – 105% and 
enabled the quantification of SY in spiked orange juice, spiked carrot juice, pediatric syrup and carbonated 
orange beverage without the need for specific reagents, costly instrumentation, or tedious extraction processes. 
The AGREEprep tool demonstrated the superior environmental impact of the developed method compared to the 
reported procedures for cloud point extraction.

1. Introduction

A wide variety of natural and synthetic coloring agents are being 
added to numerous food and pharmaceutical products to augment color 
qualities, enhance the aesthetic, and preserve the original attractiveness 
of food [1]. The food colorant industry is expanding at an annual rate of 
4.6 % and is projected to attain a value of 2.3 billion dollars in the global 
market. The color of food is regarded as the most crucial factor when 
purchasing any food item, as it is typically linked to the freshness and 
quality of the product [1]. However, numerous colorants can induce 
severe negative effects, including cancer and allergic reactions [2]. Strict 
regulations governing the utilization of approved colorants and the 
disclosure of their origins minimize health hazards associated with such 
toxic compounds, thereby safeguarding consumer safety and preserving 
product integrity through careful control of the extraction and analysis 
of food colorants [2].

Sunset yellow (SY, E110), an orange-red colored synthetic azo-dye, is 
one of the most widely used water-soluble coloring agents that has been 

used extensively in pharmaceutical and food industries. (Fig. S1A). The 
acceptable daily intake of SY is 4 mg/kg/day as decided by European 
food safety authority [3]. The ingestion of SY has been linked to some 
adverse health effects such as sensitivity reaction, hyperactivity, cellular 
immunosuppression, asthma, genetic material damage, hepatotoxicity, 
renal failure, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and po-
tential immunotoxicity [4]. Therefore, a straightforward approach is 
necessary to control SY level in the different products.

So far, some methods have been developed for determination of SY in 
food samples. These methods include electrochemical [5], chromato-
graphic [6–8], and spectroscopic methods [9–10]. Due to the complex 
nature of sample matrix, SY is usually extracted using cloud point 
extraction [11], liquid-liquid extraction (LLE) [12], and column solid 
phase extraction (SPE) [9]. However, the utilization of hazardous sol-
vents and persistent toxic organic waste make LLE and conventional SPE 
harmful to the ecosystem. Also, SPE requires well-trained personnel and 
expensive instruments. On the other hand, cloud point extraction is 
laborious and suffers from impaired reproducibility due to possible loss

quently, it is imperative to develop a simple, fast, and sustainable 
method for extraction recovery of SY from various samples. Thus, the 
costly equipment, time-consuming, and tedious extraction processes are 
substituted.

The introduction of paper-based sorbents is significant milestone in 
real-time pharmaceutical quality control and food safety assessment 
[14]. Owing to their numerous excellent capabilities, paper-based 
sorptive extraction has gained a lot of attention. These characteristics 
include being cost-effective, biodegradable and biocompatible, versa-
tile, and producing a quick response [15]. The progress of paper-based 
SPE is ascribed to its simplicity, sustainability, and affordability. Also, 
the use of existing planar material greatly simplifies the preparation of 
sorptive phases. On the other hand, the natural origin of cellulose con-
tributes to the greenness of these sorbents by affording high wettability, 
reducing the environmental hazards, and producing biodegradable 
wastes [16].

The preparation of paper-based sorbents can be performed through 
physical or chemical modification of paper’s surface with polymer [17], 
employing either dip-coating [18–19], filtration [20], or drop-casting 
[21] strategies. The dip-coating technique is based on paper immer-
sion in the coating solution for a specified time; thereafter, the excess 
coating solution is removed. In contrast, the drop-casting method in-
volves putting a drop of the coating agent directly on the paper’s surface, 
while the filtration method works by soaking the surface modifier into 
the paper’s porous surface as the coating solution is filtered.

Chitosan (CHT) (Fig. S1B) is a linear polysaccharide composed of 
varying amounts of (β1→4) linked N-acetyl-2 amino-2-deoxy-D-glucose 
(glucosamine) and 2-amino-2-deoxy-D-Glucose (N-acetyl-glucosamine) 
residues [22]. CHT is soluble in aqueous acidic media via primary amine 
protonation [23,24]. Also, in acidic medium, CHT has a considerable 
affinity towards acid dyes due to the protonated amine groups. CHT is a 
well-known polysaccharide for its ability to be cast as thin films that is 
characterized by its excellent adsorption capabilities [25]. However, the 
fragility of CHT films is a major concern affecting their integrity. 
Numerous researchers have utilized different plasticizers for maintain-
ing the flexibility and integrity of CHT membranes [26]. However, such 
chemical modifiers would lower CHT’s adsorption abilities. Thus, the 
deposition of CHT on a paper support can alleviate most of the obstacles 
encountered [27].

In this work, a novel paper-based SPE method was developed for 
extraction and spectrophotometric determination of SY. The developed 
method relies on the use of CHT-coated paper sorbent. Due to the pro-
tonated CHT, SY was effectively adsorbed on the newly formed sorbent 
in a short time allowing its determination using spectrophotometry after 
elution using an aliquot of 1 mL of elution solvent. The devised CHT- 
coated sorbent was successfully applied for SY determination in spiked 
orange juice, spiked carrot juice, pediatric syrup, and carbonated 
beverage. The developed method was proved to be green, rapid, and 
straightforward, outperforming the performance of the reported SY 
extraction methods.

2. Experimental

2.1. Materials

CHT(med. MW, Deacetylation degree of 75–85 %) was purchased 
from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Glacial acetic acid (HPLC Grade) 
was purchased from Advent Chembio (Maharashtra, India). Double 
Rings® (102) filter paper (9.0 cm diameter) was used for paper-based 
sorbent preparation. Sodium hydroxide (Laboratory Reagent Grade) 
was supplied by ISO–CHEM (Egypt). SY with purity 99 % was kindly 
supplied by sigma pharmaceutical industry (Quesna, Egypt). HPLC 
grade methanol and hydrochloric acid were obtained from Sigma- 
Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Pediatric analgesic syrup labelled to contain 
50 mg/mL paracetamol, a product of EIPICO (Tenth of Ramadan City, 

2 

Egypt), was purchased from local pharmacy (Tanta, Egypt). Carbonated 
orange beverage was obtained from local market (Tanta, Egypt). Fresh 
oranges and carrots were purchased from the local market, washed 
thoroughly with water, and processed to obtain fresh orange and carrot 
juice. Orange juice was produced by pressing fresh oranges, whereas 
carrot juice was made by blending boiled carrots with a hand blender. 
Both juices were strained to remove any particulate matter and stored in 
clean containers at 4 

C until used.

2.2. Instruments and software

UV/Vis spectra were recorded in the range of 300–600 nm on a 
JASCO v-530 UV/Vis double beam spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) 
equipped with a 1-mL microcuvette and connected to a PC operated by 
Jasco Spectra Manager (1.55.00). A JOEL JSM IT-00 scanning electron 
microscope (Tokyo, Japan) was used for acquiring SEM images and 
energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX-SEM) analysis. For water contact angle 
(WCA) measurements, a smartphone (Samsung Galaxy note 10 lite, 
Vietnam) setup was employed for photo acquisition [28]. Images of a 
100-μL water drop were captured using the single take mode (15 s), then 
transferred to a PC for processing with ImageJ 1.54 g (version 
1.8.0_345). The contact angle was measured using the Drop Analysis 
Plug-In [29], following these steps: open ImageJ, load the image, 
navigate to Image -> Type->32-bit, Plug-in- > drop-analysis - > Drop 
analysis-LB-ADSA. Hot plate magnetic stirrer (DAIHAN, Malaysia) and 
digital balance (Sartorius, Germany) were used.

2.3. Preparation of paper-based sorbent

The CHT-coated paper-based sorbent was prepared according the 
method reported by Abdella et al. [28] that were modified after Tang 
et al. [30]. Each paper piece was dipped in 5 mL of 0.6 % CHT, dissolved 
acetic acid (1 % v/v), for 2 h. The paper sheet was positioned upright to 
aid in the removal of excess CHT and was left to air dry at room tem-
◦
perature (25 
C). Then, the CHT-coated paper was cut into small pieces 
(1 × 1 cm) and stored in a dry clean container until further utilized, as 
shown in Fig. 1.

2.4. General procedure for sunset yellow determination

A stock standard solution of SY (1 mg/mL) was prepared in distilled 
water. Serial dilutions were made using the same solvent to obtain 
working standard solutions of SY between 10 - 60 μg/mL. The prepared 
paper-based sorbent sheets were placed in 2 mL of working standard SY 
solution for 20 min. Then, the sorptive paper pieces were moved to a dry 
paper sheet for 1 min and then immersed in 1 mL of solvent system 
composed of methanol: 0.1 N HCl (1:1 v/v) for 25 min (Fig. 1). Finally, 
the UV/Vis spectra of the eluate solutions were recorded between 300 – 
600 nm. Calibration curves were constructed by plotting the absorbance 
at 482 nm against the corresponding dye concentration (μg/mL).

2.5. Determination of sunset yellow in real samples

For fresh juice analysis, the homemade orange and carrot juice were 
spiked with SY. Different aliquots (0–500 μL) of 1 mg/mL stock standard 
solution of SY were separately transferred into a series of 10-mL volu-
metric flasks. The solution was made up to final volume using each juice 
to obtain spiked juice samples containing 0–50 μg/mL of SY. The solu-
tions were analyzed as per the procedure aforementioned in Section 2.4
to calculate the recovered SY concentration.

For real samples, containers of pediatric antipyretic syrup and 
carbonated orange beverage, were shaken for homogenization. An 
aliquot of 1 mL of each sample was separately transferred into a series of 
tube vessels. Then, a volume of 1 mL of standard SY (0–50 μg/mL) was 
added to each vessel. The analysis was performed as aforementioned in 
Section 2.4. SY concentration in the diluted sample solution was

3. Results and discussion

3.1. Preparation and characterization

Since the CHT layer is fundamental for SY binding onto paper sur-
face, the effectiveness of coating process was investigated using various 
characterization techniques. To prepare the paper sorbent, the paper 
was dip-coated with 0.6 % CHT for 2 h. The prepared sorptive sheets 
were then soaked in SY to evaluate the feasibility of the extraction 
process.

SEM images were acquired for uncoated paper (Fig. 2A and D), pa-
per’s surface after coating with CHT (Fig. 2B and E), and coated paper 
after SY binding (Fig. 2C and F). The unmodified paper showed a porous 

surface displaying fibrous cellulose structure that was changed to 
smoother surface with less voids after coating with CHT. The deposition 
of CHT on paper’s surface could be attributed to the electrostatic 
interaction between the negative charges of cellulose and the positively 
charged CHT [28]. Besides, the binding of SY to paper’s surface 
increased the surface roughness and irregularities, as demonstrated by 
the SEM images (Fig. 2C and F) [31].

To confirm the effectiveness of the coating step, elemental analysis 
was done on the uncoated cellulose fiber surface, paper coated with CHT 
and coated paper after SY binding (Fig. S2). The elemental composition 
of the uncoated paper revealed carbon and oxygen as the primary ele-
ments (Fig. S2A). While the CHT-coated paper showed a nitrogen 
component originating from amine groups of CHT (Fig. S2B). This 
would suggest effective paper coating with a CHT layer. Also, an addi-
tional sulfur component was found in coated paper after adsorption of 
SY indicating effective binding (Fig. S2C).
